sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient is a 31-year-old male who presented with a 3-month history of cough, initially diagnosed as bronchitis by his primary care physician, but a chest X-ray and subsequent CT scan revealed a 2-centimeter nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe.
He reports that the cough started after a physically demanding activity of loading horses and has since resolved with periods of exacerbation.
He denies any pain, shortness of breath, or fever.
The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly, and a history of smoking that ended 7 years ago.
His family history includes breast cancer in his mother, but no lung cancer.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly, and a history of smoking that ended 7 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient denies any pain, shortness of breath, or fever.
Current medications: Methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are within normal limits, with a blood pressure of 124/76, heart rate of 70, respiration rate of 16, oxygen saturation of 98% on room air, and afebrile.
Physical exam: The patient's lungs are clear and equal bilateral, with no evidence of wheezing or crackles.
The heart is regular rate and rhythm, with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs.
The extremities are pink, warm, and dry, with no edema.
The patient has some swelling in the knuckles, which is consistent with his rheumatoid arthritis.

Test Results: The patient had a chest CT scan that revealed a 2-centimeter nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, which appears smooth in appearance and no evidence of emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule, which requires further evaluation and management.
Problem list: Lung nodule; Rheumatoid arthritis; History of smoking.
Plan:
I will order a pulmonary function test (PFT) to establish a baseline for the patient's lung function.
I will also schedule a PET CT to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.
The patient will undergo video-assisted thoracoscopy to remove the nodule, which will be performed under general anesthesia and will take approximately 1.5 hours.
The patient will be admitted to the hospital for one night and will have a chest tube inserted until the following day.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in a few minutes to sign the paperwork and begin the treatment process.
